food_subgroup: Stir-fried leafy vegetables with fermented condiments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for stir-fried kangkung (Ipomoea aquatica) with belacan. Closest parent is a stir-fried leafy vegetable dish. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried kangkung (water spinach, Ipomoea aquatica) with belacan (fermented shrimp paste), garlic, chilli, soy sauce, and oyster sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0BDF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for stir-fried leafy vegetables; no exact code exists for this specific Southeast Asian preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker centre plate of stir-fried kangkung with belacan is approximately 200g of cooked water spinach with sauce, typically shared as a vegetable side dish for 1–2 persons.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried Kangkung Belacan — HPB Singapore (Foodo) / USDA FoodData Central water spinach reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for stir-fried kangkung belacan. Micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked water spinach (Ipomoea aquatica) and adjusted for belacan, soy sauce, and oyster sauce additions. Sodium reflects the combined contribution of belacan, soy sauce, and oyster sauce as reported in HPB data.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — stir-fried leafy vegetables with fermented condiments; analogous estimate for kangkung belacan (GI ~15–25)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried kangkung belacan in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~20 based on analogous stir-fried leafy green vegetable dishes with negligible starch content. Kangkung is predominantly water, fibre, and micronutrients with very low digestible carbohydrate (5.2g/100g), supporting a low GI classification. GL per 200g serving = (20 × 5.2 × 2) / 100 = 2.08, rounded to 2.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (568mg/100g) from belacan, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; a 200g serving delivers approximately 1,136mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 100g or request reduced belacan and soy sauce when ordering; the potassium content (295mg/100g) from kangkung provides partial compensatory benefit but does not offset the high sodium load at standard serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~20) and negligible glycemic load per serving (GL 2.1); high dietary fibre (2.2g/100g) slows glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried kangkung with belacan is an excellent vegetable choice for patients with Type 2 Diabetes — the very low GI (~20), minimal digestible carbohydrate (5.2g/100g), and high fibre content produce negligible postprandial glucose impact, and pairing it with higher-GI main dishes helps moderate the overall meal glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (568mg/100g) is a cardiovascular risk factor; however, the dish is low in saturated fat (0.5g/100g), contains zero trans fat, and provides cardioprotective micronutrients including potassium (295mg/100g), folate (35mcg/100g), and vitamin C (22mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the dish's low saturated fat, zero trans fat, and high potassium and folate content are beneficial, but the high sodium from belacan and sauces should be moderated — requesting less sauce and smaller portions (100g) can reduce sodium intake to an acceptable level while retaining the nutritional benefits of the kangkung.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin A (255mcg RAE/100g), vitamin C (22mg/100g), folate (35mcg/100g), and anti-inflammatory compounds from garlic (allicin) and chilli (capsaicin)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Stir-fried kangkung with belacan is clinically beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its high antioxidant and anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile, including beta-carotene-derived vitamin A, vitamin C for collagen synthesis, and folate for DNA repair and immune modulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Kangkung with Belacan (Kangkung Goreng Belacan) is one of Singapore's and Malaysia's most universally eaten hawker vegetable dishes, found at every Chinese cze char stall, Malay rice stall, and mixed hawker centre. Kangkung (Ipomoea aquatica — water spinach or morning glory) is a semi-aquatic tropical leafy vegetable with hollow stems and arrowhead-shaped tender leaves, prized for its mild, slightly sweet flavour and satisfying crunch that persists even after high-heat stir-frying. The cooking technique is fast and furious: a screaming-hot wok is essential to achieve wok hei (the smoky, slightly charred aroma unique to high-heat wok cooking). Belacan — dried, toasted, and pounded fermented shrimp paste — is sizzled with garlic and fresh or dried chilli until deeply aromatic, then the kangkung is added in a single thrust and tossed for barely 2–3 minutes until wilted but still vibrant green. Soy sauce and oyster sauce complete the seasoning, creating a glossy, intensely savoury, umami-rich sauce that clings to every leaf and stem. Nutritionally, this dish is extraordinary for its calorie density: only 58 kcal per 100g, yet it delivers exceptionally high vitamin A (255mcg RAE/100g from beta-carotene), meaningful vitamin C (22mg/100g), good folate (35mcg/100g), and useful calcium (52mg/100g) and iron (2.2mg/100g). The glycemic index is very low (~20) with a negligible glycemic load per serving (GL 2.1), making it ideal for diabetes management. The primary clinical caution is high sodium (568mg/100g) from the belacan and sauces, which makes it unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by hypertension patients. Requesting reduced sauce and belacan is the standard mitigation strategy. The dish is halal, dairy-free, and contains crustacean and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: is kangkung belacan good for weight loss
meal_context: shared vegetable dish at Chinese dinner

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific dish (stir-fried kangkung belacan), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for water spinach micronutrients. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous leafy vegetable entries with documented rationale. All values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for stir-fried kangkung belacan is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the cooked dish as prepared is not reported in the primary source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): kangkung is a plant-based vegetable with no vitamin B12; the trace contribution from belacan is below detection threshold and reported as 0 in HPB data. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no vitamin D is present in any ingredient of this dish per HPB and USDA sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation. GI value of 20 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database stir-fried leafy vegetable entries; no direct GI measurement for kangkung belacan exists in the database. Sodium of 568mg/100g reflects the combined contribution of belacan, soy sauce, and oyster sauce as reported in HPB data — this is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients.
